text:
There are two link forms in the code. One is robust, one isn't:
- `[[slug]]` wikilinks — topic-agnostic, resolved by slug. Survive moves.
- `[Name](slug.md)` See-Also links **and** the `_index.md` table hrefs (`[slug](slug.md)`) — these are **relative paths**.  [...] **The contrast is the point.** In the flat design, `topic` is a pure attribute and `_index.md` is a *derived view* that regroups for free — so re-topicing is a one-line metadata edit with zero moves, zero link rewrites, zero resolver changes. That's why the team shipped it that way. Folders aren't impossible — you'd make `[[slug]]` the canonical link form, turn `guide_path` into a recursive resolver, and centralize rename-on-retopic — but each of those is a place to get it wrong, and because topic is *mutable*, the churn is ongoing, not a one-time migration. [...] - `[Name](slug.md)` See-Also links **and** the `_index.md` table hrefs (`[slug](slug.md)`) — these are **relative paths**. 

Once guides live in `docs/wiki/<topic>/`, a See-Also from guide A (topic X) to guide B (topic Y) must become `../Y/B.md`, and the root index needs `X/A.md`. When B later moves Y→Z, **every inbound `../Y/B.md` across the whole wiki is now a dead link.** So one retopic has to rename N files *and* rewrite every inbound link to each moved file. Today it does neither.
